does: This paper investigates prompt injection vulnerabilities in agentic software reverse engineering systems, demonstrating how adversaries can embed malicious prompts into binary executable source code that influence AI agent behavior during analysis. The authors develop detection methods to identify prompt injection strings appearing in decompiler output, then explore obfuscation techniques attackers might use to evade detection, followed by defensive countermeasures against these obfuscations. The work provides empirical analysis of attack and defense strategies, establishing concrete security risks in agentic reverse engineering workflows and offering practical detection approaches necessary for safe deployment of such systems in production cyber security operations.
connects: This work builds on emerging research in adversarial attacks against large language model-based agents, extending threat models previously explored in prompt injection literature to the specific domain of automated software reverse engineering. It connects to prior work on code-based adversarial examples and LLM robustness, while also drawing from traditional reverse engineering security concerns and binary analysis vulnerabilities. The paper responds to growing adoption of AI agents in cyber security tasks and the need to understand supply chain risks where executables containing hidden prompts could compromise analysis pipelines, combining insights from prompt injection defenses with malware analysis and code obfuscation techniques.
enables: This research enables more secure deployment of agentic reverse engineering tools by establishing baseline detection and defense mechanisms against injection attacks. It opens directions for developing more robust prompting strategies, semantic-level detection approaches, and adversarial training methods specific to code analysis agents. The work points toward needed security hardening of AI agents used in cyber security, informing threat modeling for supply chain attacks where malicious binaries target analytical AI systems. Future work could explore automated defenses, formal verification of agent robustness, and organizational protocols for safely integrating agentic tools into security operations while maintaining human oversight and verification of critical findings.
